coredata.dc:description: The widespread use of Cd-based quantum dots (Cd-QDs) has led to their inevitable release into the environment, and the prevalent iron oxides and natural organic matter (NOM) are the key factors affecting the environmental behavior and fate of Cd-QDs. However, the impact of NOM adsorbed on iron oxides on the behavior of Cd-QDs with iron oxides and the mechanism of its interaction are not clear. In this study, two kinds of water-soluble QDs (CdSe QDs and core-shell CdSe/ZnS QDs) were selected to study the aggregation and adsorption behavior on goethite (Goe) and goethite-humic acid/fulvic acid composites (Goe-HA/FA). Aggregation kinetics and adsorption experiments between QDs and Goe(-HA/FA), characterization, and extended Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek (XDLVO) theory calculations indicated that electrostatic interaction was the dominant force for QDs adsorption on Goe(-HA/FA). HA/FA changed the surface charge of Goe and increased the electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance between the particles, which in turn inhibited the adsorption of QDs on Goe. Besides, unsubstituted aromatic carbons, carboxy carbons, and carbonyl carbons played an important role in the adsorption process, and chemisorption occurred between QDs and Goe(-HA/FA). Our findings are important for better assessing the transport, fate, and potential environmental impacts and risks of Cd-QDs in iron-rich environments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Zeta potentials of individual QDs (5 mg/L), Goe, Goe-HA/FA (100 mg/L), QDs-Goe, and QDs–Goe-HA/FA (QDs: 1 mg/L, Goe and Goe-HA/FA: 100 mg/L) suspensions at pH 5.5 and 7.5 in 1, 10, and 100 mM NaNO3.

Homoaggregation of CdSe/ZnS QDs at 5 mg/L and Goe at 100 mg/L, and heteroaggregation of CdSe/ZnS QDs at 1 mg/L, Goe and Goe-HA/FA at 100 mg/L. Experiments were conducted at 10 mM NaNO3, pH 5.5 (a,c,e,g), and pH 7.5 (b,d,f,h).
Calculated DLVO and XDLVO interaction energy profiles between QDs and Goe/Goe-HA/FA based on NaNO3 concentrations of 1, 10, and 100 mM at pH 5.5 and 7.5.
The adsorption kinetic models and adsorption isotherm models for CdSe QDs (a-b), CdSe/ZnS QDs (c-d), and Cd2+ (e-f).

(a) DF-TEM images of Goe after CdSe/ZnS QDs adsorption; (b-e) elemental mapping of Fe, Cd, and Zn; (f-g) Micro–XRF maps show the selected element distribution of Cd and Fe in the Goe-HA after adsorbing CdSe/ZnS QDs at pH 5.5 and 10 mM NaNO3. The scanning step is 5 µm × 5 µm, and the total map area is 100 × 100 µm2. (h) The correlation between counts of Cd with Fe; (i) XPS spectra of C1s and O1s; (j) FTIR spectra of Goe and Goe-HA/FA before and after adsorption of QDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread use of Cd-based quantum dots (Cd-QDs) has led to their inevitable release into the environment, and the prevalent iron oxides and natural organic matter (NOM) are the key factors affecting the environmental behavior and fate of Cd-QDs. However, the impact of NOM adsorbed on iron oxides on the behavior of Cd-QDs with iron oxides and the mechanism of its interaction are not clear. In this study, two kinds of water-soluble QDs (CdSe QDs and core-shell CdSe/ZnS QDs) were selected to study the aggregation and adsorption behavior on goethite (Goe) and goethite-humic acid/fulvic acid composites (Goe-HA/FA). Aggregation kinetics and adsorption experiments between QDs and Goe(-HA/FA), characterization, and extended Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek (XDLVO) theory calculations indicated that electrostatic interaction was the dominant force for QDs adsorption on Goe(-HA/FA). HA/FA changed the surface charge of Goe and increased the electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance between the particles, which in turn inhibited the adsorption of QDs on Goe. Besides, unsubstituted aromatic carbons, carboxy carbons, and carbonyl carbons played an important role in the adsorption process, and chemisorption occurred between QDs and Goe(-HA/FA). Our findings are important for better assessing the transport, fate, and potential environmental impacts and risks of Cd-QDs in iron-rich environments.

The continuous production and use of cadmium-based quantum dots (Cd-QDs) leads to their unavoidable release into the environment, where natural organic matter (NOM) and iron oxides could affect their fate. In this study, we demonstrated that electrostatic interaction dominated the adsorption of Cd-QDs on goethite, while NOM adsorbed on the surface of goethite inhibited the adsorption of Cd-QDs by changing its surface charge and increasing the electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance. Our findings provide a theoretical basis for understanding the interactions of Cd-QDs with iron oxides and iron oxide-organic matter adsorption composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are a new type of fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals with a diameter of approximately 2 to 10 nm, whose typical structural makeup consists of a semiconductor core surrounded by wider-bandgap materials as a protective layer (such as ZnS shell) or an organic termination agent [28]. Different from other metal-based nanoparticles (MNPs), QDs have unique properties due to their surface effects, size effects, quantum confined effects, and macroscopic quantum tunneling effects. As a result, QDs are extensively used in the production of optoelectronic devices, biomedical devices, and sensors [24]. Cadmium-based QDs (Cd-QDs) such as CdSe, CdTe, and CdS have received the most attention due to the advantages of strong fluorescence, stable luminescence, good biocompatibility, and easy synthesis. However, more and more Cd-QDs are released into the environment in the process of production, transportation, and use with the gradual expansion of the production and application scale [13]. Additionally, toxic metal ions (Cd2+) may be released during the weathering process of Cd-QDs, which poses additional environmental risks. The toxicity of Cd-QDs to aquatic and terrestrial organisms has been extensively evaluated, but their environmental impact remains uncertain, which depends on their fate, persistence, and bioavailability. Therefore, it is essential to research how QDs migrate and transform in the environment.

Whether QDs are dispersed in water or adsorbed on soil, they may undergo various transformations after releasing into the environment, including aggregation, sedimentation, dissolution, or up-taken by animals, plants, and microorganisms [22]. The environmental behavior and final fate of QDs are closely related to specific environmental conditions such as pH value, ionic strength (IS), and natural organic matter (NOM). Changes in pH value can alter the zeta potential of QDs and modify the repulsive or attractive forces with adjacent particles, while protonation or deprotonation of capping ligands can also cause aggregation of QDs, which in turn affects the stability of QDs [52]. The ubiquitous NOM in the environment can reduce the aggregation and increase the stability of QDs suspensions through electrostatic and repulsive spatial interactions. It has been found that two typical NOMs, humic acid (HA) and fulvic acid (FA), could improve the stability of trioctylphosphine oxide-capped CdSe QDs in aqueous solution, and the QDs were transferred from the organic phase to the aqueous phase by ligand exchange and complexation with NOM [27]. In addition, the interaction between QDs and components in the environment may also significantly affect the transport, effects, and fate of QDs. Liu et al. found that minerals in the environment, such as goethite, kaolinite, montmorillonite, and attapulgite, can adsorb carbon dots and affect the potential fate of carbon dots in the natural environment [25,26]. It has been investigated that electrostatic interactions dominate the adsorption process of carbon dots, and hydrogen-bonding interactions and Lewis acid-base interactions also favor the aggregation of carbon dots. However, few studies have been reported on the interactions between Cd-QDs and natural mineral particles, which may be destabilized by heterogeneous aggregation between Cd-QDs and minerals.

Iron (hydr)oxides and NOM are two important reactive substances in the environment and are widely distributed in soil, sediment, and aquatic systems. Because of their variable surface charge, large specific surface area, and abundance of surface functional groups, iron (hydr)oxides not only have the potential to influence biogeochemical processes in other substances but also regulate the migration and transformation of pollutants [4]. Besides, NOM containing a large number of surface functional groups will change the surface properties of MNPs by altering the surface charge, reactivity, and aggregation behavior, which in turn affects their environmental behavior [17]. Many studies have shown that iron (hydr)oxides and NOM play an important role in mediating the stability, aggregation, and reactivity of co-existing MNPs discharged into the soil or aqueous environment [19,36,41,54]. Wang et al. [41] explored the interaction between iron oxides and silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) and found that electrostatic attraction played a dominant role in the adsorption of Ag NPs by iron oxides and inhibited Ag NPs dissolution during aggregation. Zhang et al. [53] investigated that polystyrene nanoplastics had a strong electrostatic attraction with positive goethite and magnetite. Li et al. [19] found that the concentration of HA was highly correlated with the stability of CeO2 NPs. A low concentration of HA was conducive to the accumulation of NPs, while a high concentration of HA made NPs more stable due to electrostatic repulsion and steric hindrance.

Significantly, in natural environments, iron (hydr)oxides and NOM rarely exist as separate components, and the acidic oxygen-containing functional groups on NOM (mainly -COOH) could strongly bind to the hydroxyl groups (-OH) on iron (hydr)oxides [37]. Iron (hydr)oxides usually form composites with organic matter through adsorption (organic matter binds with previously formed iron (hydr)oxides) or co-precipitation (organic matter participates in the iron (hydr)oxides formation process) [2]. At present, most of the studies mainly focus on the interactions between iron (hydr)oxides and MNPs or between NOM and MNPs, but few studies have paid attention to how the composites of iron (hydr)oxides coupled with NOM (Fe-OM) interact with MNPs. The adsorption of NOM can change the surface charge and properties of iron (hydr)oxides, which in turn affects the heteroaggregation and adsorption behavior between Fe-OM and MNPs. Therefore, studies on the interactions and adsorption behavior between Fe-OM and MNPs are needed. Zhao et al. [54] revealed that the presence of extracellular polymeric substances could greatly inhibit the heteroaggregation between CeO2 NPs and positively charged goethite by enhancing the electrostatic repulsion between NPs and goethite. However, little information is available on the aggregation and adsorption behavior between Fe-OM and QDs, and the driving force of the interaction between QDs and Fe-OM deserves further investigation.

It is essential to research the interaction between QDs and Fe-OM. In this study, goethite was employed as the model iron (hydr)oxides because it is not only widely distributed, but also stable in natural soil and water environments [46]. To better investigate the contribution of Fe-OM to the aggregation of CdSe QDs, three iron oxides including goethite (Goe), goethite-HA adsorption composites (Goe-HA), and goethite-FA adsorption composites (Goe-FA) were used to simulate the aggregation behavior and adsorption of CdSe QDs under environmentally relevant solution chemistries (IS and pH). Meanwhile, the aggregation kinetics between CdSe QDs and Fe-OM were determined using the time-resolved dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique, and the interparticle interaction forces were calculated by Derjaguin-Landau-Verwey-Overbeek (DLVO) and extended DLVO (XDLVO) theory. Besides, the interactions between QDs and Goe(-HA/FA) were investigated using batch adsorption experiments. In addition, transmission electron microscopy-energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (TEM-EDS), Micro X-ray fluorescence (μ-XRF), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were applied to reveal the interparticle interaction mechanism between CdSe QDs and Fe-OM. Our study will provide new insights into the stability, mobility, and environmental fate of CdSe QDs in iron (hydr)oxides-rich and organic matter-rich environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdSe QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA
ce:section.ce:para: MPA-CdSe QDs and MPA-CdSe/ZnS QDs were prepared based on previous literature with some modifications [55,56]. Goe was synthesized by coprecipitation [12], and Goe-HA/FA was prepared by mixing Goe and HA/FA stock solution. The chemicals and detailed synthesis processes of QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA were shown in Supporting Information.

The aggregation kinetics were studied by DLS. Separate experiments were conducted to investigate the homoaggregation kinetics of CdSe QDs, CdSe/ZnS QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA, as well as the heteroaggregation kinetics of QDs with goethite or adsorption composites. Different concentrations of NaNO3 solution (1, 10, and 100 mg/L) were prepared as background electrolytes. The pH of the solution was environmentally relevant and was maintained at 5.5 and 7.5, respectively [35]. 10 mM morpholine ethanesulfonic acid (MES) and 3-moroline propyl sulfonic acid (MOPS) were added as buffers, respectively. The Dh and ζ-potentials of QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA were measured under different pH and IS conditions. In addition, the ζ-potentials of QDs under different pH were determined at a background solution NaNO3 concentrations of 10 mM.

QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA powder were suspended in ultrapure water and ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Fresh stock suspensions were prepared before performing each experiment. The homoaggregation kinetics were studied at goethite (or adsorption composites) and QDs concentrations of 100 mg/L and 5 mg/L, respectively, and those of heteroaggregation at concentrations of 100 mg/L and 1 mg/L, respectively. For DLS analysis, the samples were mixed with the background solution rapidly and placed into the DLS sample cell immediately for measurement, and recorded every 75 s for a period of 31.25 min, thus obtaining the kinetic curve of Dh with time. The QDs concentrations were 5-fold higher in the homoaggregation experiment to ensure sufficient light intensity for DLS measurements.

The initial aggregation rate constant (k) was calculated for the time period from the initial aggregation (t0) to the time Dh exceeded 1.5 Dh,0, which was proportional to (dDh(t)/dt):

where N0 was the initial particle concentration. Dh(t) was the average hydrodynamic diameter at time t. The value of dDh(t)/dt was obtained through linear least squares regression analysis.

The DLVO theory was commonly used to describe the interaction forces between particles, which generally included electrostatic repulsion and van der Waals attraction due to the overlap of the diffusing electrical double layers. Since HA and FA adsorbed by iron oxide form a molecular coating with a certain thickness, which increases the distance between the particles, it is also necessary to take into account the steric repulsion of the organic matter in the calculation of the interaction energy. Therefore, the extended DLVO (XDLVO) theory was introduced for the calculation, including the interaction force in the DLVO theory and steric hindrance [40]. The total interaction energy (Vtotal) was defined as the sum of the van der Waals interaction energy (Vvdw), electric double-layer interaction energy (Vedl), osmotic repulsion (Vosm), and elastic-steric repulsion (Velas) interaction energies [30,51], and the details of the calculations were provided in the Supporting Information.

The experiment of synchrotron-based µ-XRF was carried out at the BL15U1 beamline of the Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility (SSRF). The stored ring electron energy was 3.5 GeV and the current intensity was up to 300 mA. A CCD camera was used to select the sample area, and the excitation energy was set to 13 KeV. Fluorescence signals of elements were collected by Si(III) solid-state multi-detector, and the spot size was 5 µm × 5 µm, and fluorescence signals of Cd, Fe, Se, S, and Zn were collected [6].

Excel 2016 was used for preliminary screening of data, and OriginPro 2023 was used for drawing and model fitting. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The experimental data was shown as the average value ± standard deviation (SD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of QDs, goethite, and adsorption composites
ce:section.ce:para: MPA-CdSe QDs and MPA-CdSe/ZnS QDs were spherical in shape, with particle sizes of 4.40 ± 0.77 nm and 4.63 ± 0.72 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). The results of XRD patterns revealed that the QDs had three characteristic peaks of CdSe, mainly 2θ = 25.3°, 42.0°, and 49.7°, which corresponded to the (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes, respectively. The diffraction peaks were slightly shifted after the coating of ZnS, which might be owing to ZnS using the original CdSe QDs as growth sites and wrapped around their exterior to reduce the defects on the surface of CdSe [15,31]. FTIR spectra were carried out to determine the surface functional groups of QDs. The wide peaks at 3217 cm−1 and 3257 cm−1 corresponded to O–H stretching vibration, indicating the presence of water molecules because QDs were synthesized in the aqueous phase [5]. The peak at 1632 cm−1 was due to the CO stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid group from MPA. The peaks at 1010 cm−1 and 1066 cm−1 corresponded to CO stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1111 cm−1 and 1354 cm−1 were mainly due to COC asymmetrical stretching and CH2 wagging, respectively [38]. Finally, the absence of –SH around 2600 cm−1 indicated the presence of Cd-SR- in the QDs rather than MPA [43]. The UV absorption peaks of QDs were wide, and the emission wavelengths were all 458 nm with symmetric and narrow half-peak widths at the excitation wavelength of 229 nm, indicating that the QDs were dispersed and homogeneous in size. The fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced after coating the ZnS shell, revealing that ZnS was successfully encapsulated into the CdSe core.

Goe formed an acicular crystal with a rough surface and small particle size with an average length of 220–300 nm and an average width of 30–40 nm. Goe had a uniform size distribution and a BET-specific surface area of 112.23 m2/g. The XRD pattern showed specific peaks well-matched with goethite with good crystallinity. According to FTIR spectra (Fig. S1h), Goe and Goe-HA/FA contained a stretching band at 635 cm−1 represented Fe–O vibration, bending bands at 890 cm−1 and 793 cm−1 represented Fe–O–OH vibrations, and an intense band at 3000 ∼ 3500 cm−1 represented bulk O–H stretching vibration, which were the characteristic absorption peaks for goethite [29]. Compared to bare Goe, the symmetric and anti-symmetric CH2 stretching vibrations around 2850 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 were increased in peak intensity, which might be due to the adsorption of HA or FA onto the iron oxide surface [32]. The intensity of stretching vibrations of the O–H at 3435 cm−1, 3415 cm−1, and 3120 cm−1 declined or disappeared after the adsorption of HA or FA. In addition, the symmetric COO− band reflected by the peak at 1380 cm−1 became weakened, and the antisymmetric stretching peaks of COO− groups at 1625 cm−1 and 1636 cm−1 were red-shifted with a decrease in peak intensity [11]. It indicated that the Goe-HA/FA were mainly formed by ligand exchange between the -COO of HA or FA and the -OH on the surface of goethite [23,49]. Based on the BET model, the SSA of adsorption composites increased compared with pure goethite after HA adsorption and decreased after FA adsorption. It might be due to FA with a smaller molecular weight being more easily adsorbed by Goe (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA
ce:section.ce:para: The ζ-potentials of QDs were around − 12 mV, and the Dh of CdSe QDs and CdSe/ZnS QDs were 274 ± 42 nm and 189 ± 29 nm in ultrapure water, respectively (Fig. S5(c)). The Dh of QDs in solution was larger than the particle size obtained from TEM characterization due to the agglomeration effect. The ζ-potential and Dh of Cd-QDs in the background solution (NaNO3 = 10 mM) at different pH conditions are shown in Fig. S6. As the pH increased from 3 to 9, the ζ-potentials of CdSe QD and CdSe/ZnS QDs decreased from 12.27 and 9.68 mV to − 11.14 and − 19.96 mV, respectively. The ζ-potentials of QDs were significantly altered by pH due to changes in the nature and density of the surface charge of QDs. The point of zero charge (PZC) of CdSe QDs and CdSe/ZnS QDs were 4.00 (PZCCdSe) and 4.02 (PZCCdSe/ZnS), respectively, indicating that QDs were positively charged at pH < 4 and negatively charged at pH > 4. Furthermore, when the solution pH was close to PZC, the Dh of CdSe QDs and CdSe/ZnS QDs increased to 1462.69 and 1610.09 nm, respectively. In the pH range of 5–9, the ζ-potentials of CdSe QDs and CdSe/ZnS QDs ranged from − 5 to − 10 mV and from − 10 to − 20 mV, respectively, indicating that the QDs were unstable and easily aggregated in background solutions. When at pH 4 or 5, the particle surface potential was near the PZC, which resulted in poor particle stability and the easy formation of aggregates with large particle sizes. The absolute value of ζ-potential of QDs gradually increased, and the Dh gradually decreased, so that the formation of aggregates diminished when pH was far from the isoelectric point. With increasing absolute ζ-potential, the colloidal system was more stable and the repulsive force between colloidal particles increased [33]. The ζ-potentials of Cd-QDs were negative for the pH (pH = 5.5 and 7.5) and ionic strength (NaNO3 =1,10 and 100 mM) ranges used in this study. For CdSe/ZnS QDs, the negative charge both increased as IS increased from 1 to 10 mM at pH 5.5 and as IS increased from 10 to 100 mM at pH 7.5 ( Fig. 1 and Fig. S6). This abnormal increase in electrophoretic mobility (i.e., ζ-potential) might be due to the adsorption of co-ions (i.e., negative ions in this case) on the hydrophobic surface fraction of the QDs, similar to previous findings [7,10].

The ζ-potentials of Goe and Goe-HA/FA at different pH conditions were shown in Fig. S1i. The PZC of Goe was 8.61, and that of Goe-HA and Goe-FA decreased to about 5.2 (PZCGoe-HA= 5.21, PZCGoe-FA=5.25) after adsorption of organic matter. It was due to the fact that pure HA and FA were typically negatively charged, and their adsorption on Goe neutralized part of the positive charge on the surface of Goe [3]. Under acidic conditions (pH < 7), Goe was more stable with a ζ-potential around 30 mV than that of Goe-HA/FA, but the opposite was under alkaline conditions (pH > 7) where the Goe-HA/FA were more stable with a ζ-potential around − 26 mV than that of Goe (ζ-potential around −10 mV). The absolute value of ζ-potential increased gradually with increasing pH after the adsorption of organic matter, which was caused by the deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of HA/FA with increasing pH and the creation of a significant amount of negative charges [39]. Carboxyl groups were the key contributors to the deprotonation at pH 2 - 5, while at pH > 5, other functional groups such as hydroxyl and phenolic hydroxyl groups would further deprotonate to produce more negative charges [50]. In addition, as IS increased, the charge shielding effect increased [8], which resulted in a decrease in the absolute value of the ζ-potential of Goe-HA/FA, as well as its stability (Fig. 1). Goe was always positively charged, while QDs were consistently negatively charged at pH 5.5 and 7.5. Therefore, electrostatic interaction might be the main mechanism between QDs and Goe, which was more favorable for homoaggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation of QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA
Homoaggregation of QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA
ce:section.ce:para: The homoaggregation kinetic process of QDs was shown in Fig. S7. At pH 5.5, the Dh of CdSe QDs under all three IS conditions increased significantly with time and obvious agglomeration occurred. It was owing to the ζ-potential of CdSe QDs were all less than − 5 mV (Fig. 1a) that close to PZCCdSe, which was not enough to keep them stable, and rapid aggregation occurred. At pH 7.5, the ζ-potential of CdSe QDs was far from that of PZCCdSe and the aggregation rate was slower. Goe showed significant aggregation at pH 7.5 (Fig. S8), and the ζ-potential was less than 10 mV for all three IS conditions, which was close to that of PZCGoe. After the adsorption of HA and FA, the particle surface changed from positively charged to negatively charged, and the stability was improved. At pH 5.5, the Dh of Goe-HA/FA increased compared to Goe due to the binding of the carboxyl group of HA to the Fe atoms on the surface of Goe; moreover, the formation of hydrogen bonds between the organic matter molecules in Goe-HA/FA, resulted in the interconnection of Goe-HA/FA [46]. It is noteworthy that the Dh of Goe-HA/FA decreased at pH 7.5 with low IS, which was due to the rapid desorption of partial organic matter on the composites after the increase in pH [1]. The increase in Dh of QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA with increasing IS was attributed to the fact that the increase in IS could lead to compression of the double electric layer and reduce the electrostatic repulsion between particles, which resulted in a decreased energy barrier and intensified aggregation of particles (Fig. S9) [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heteroaggregation of QDs, Goe, and Goe-HA/FA
ce:section.ce:para: Actually, the concentration of QDs was generally several orders of magnitude lower than that of iron oxides in the natural water and soil, so the aggregation of QDs with Goe and Goe-HA/FA dominated the migration and fate of QDs. As shown in Fig. S10, the heteroaggregation behavior was consistent with the pattern of Goe and Goe-HA/FA, and the measured values of Dh were largely contributed by Goe and Goe-HA/FA. The Dh of QDs-Goe and QDs-Goe-HA/FA at the initial time was larger than that of the single system, indicating that the aggregation of QDs occurred rapidly when they were mixed with Goe and Goe-HA/FA.

To further investigate the heteroaggregation of QDs with Goe and Goe-HA/FA, representative aggregation kinetic curves (10 mM NaNO3) were selected for analysis. As shown in Fig. 2, at pH 5.5, the initial aggregation rate (k) of Goe-QDs was 0.96 nm/s, which was larger than that of the single system (k = 0.59 nm/s). It was attributed to the heteroaggregation between Goe with a positive surface charge and QDs with a negative surface charge. Moreover, after adsorption of organic matter, the initial aggregation rates of Goe-HA-QDs and Goe-/FA-QDs were 0.92 and 1.69 nm/s, respectively, which was smaller than that of Goe-HA/FA (2.10 and 3.06 nm/s). Negatively charged QDs were introduced into the mixture (ζ-potential of QDs was −8.20), whereas Goe-HA/FA had ζ-potentials of − 0.20 and 1.21 mV, respectively, under the experimental condition (Fig. 1). The adsorption of QDs by the Goe-HA and Goe-FA added more negative charges to their surface, and thus the initial aggregation rate decreased. Besides, the adsorbed organic matter could also increase the electrostatic repulsion between particles, which led to a decrease in the non-homogeneous aggregation rate. However, the opposite results were shown when at pH 7.5, and the initial aggregation rates of Goe-HA/FA-QDs (3.00 and 2.02 nm/s) were slightly greater than that of Goe-HA/FA (2.92 and 1.15 nm/s). Although partially desorbed HA and FA were able to reduce the interparticle aggregation by increasing the electrostatic repulsive force and spatial potential resistance between QDs and Goe-HA/FA, the Dh of the QDs and Goe-HA/FA mixture gradually increased with time, indicating that QDs were able to undergo heteroaggregation with Goe-HA/FA in the presence of HA/FA [14,42].

In general, the classical DLVO theory is widely used to elaborate on the interactions between particles, but other non-DLVO forces, such as spatial interactions, hydrogen bonding, and chemical bonding, can also be involved in interparticle aggregation [18]. In this study, goethite adsorbed HA and FA, which changed the particle size of Goe. Also, the spatial repulsion energy caused by organic matter needed to be considered in the calculation of the interaction energy between QDs and Goe. Therefore, the extended DLVO (XDLVO) theory was used to calculate the interaction energy between Goe-HA/FA and QDs, including not only the electric double layer interaction energy and van der Waals interaction energy in the DLVO theory but also the steric hindrance caused by HA and FA. According to the DLVO and XDLVO theoretical calculations ( Fig. 3), the interaction energy curves of Goe-QDs were subject to electrostatic attraction, and the maximum total potential energy was negative under all conditions, indicating a favorable attachment between QDs and Goe. DLVO interactions were governed by van der Waals forces and electrostatic interactions, and the electrostatic repulsion between QDs and Goe was too low to shield the van der Waals and electrostatic gravitational forces [42]. After the adsorption of HA and FA, the maximum total potential energy existed at pH 7.5 (CdSe QDs: Φmax = 1.09–84.80 kT and 1.19–51.51 kT, and CdSe/ZnS QDs: Φmax = 2.38–9.08 kT and 0.88–5.02 kT), which was mainly influenced by the electrostatic repulsion of negative surface charges. In general, the diffusion of particles can only overcome the energy barrier of a few kT, and the presence of an energy barrier of about 10 kT or higher between particles can suppress the aggregation [34,42,9]. At pH 7.5, the energy potential barriers of CdSe QDs and Goe-HA/FA were both greater than 10 kT at lower IS conditions, and the particles were more stable. In contrast, the energy potential barriers of both CdSe/ZnS QDs and Goe-HA/FA were less than 10 kT, indicating that the particles were more likely to aggregate with each other. The DLVO and XDLVO theories predicted the heteroaggregation between QDs and Goe (-HA/FA) in agreement with the measurement of aggregation kinetics, which could well explain the mechanism by which the adsorbed HA and FA on Goe inhibited the heteroaggregation between QDs and Goe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QDs adsorbed on Goe and GoeHA/FA
ce:section.ce:para: We used adsorption kinetics to determine whether the pseudo-first-order model or the pseudo-second-order model would effectively fit the adsorption performances of QDs on Goe and Goe-HA/FA ( Fig. 4). Detailed information about the pseudo-first-order model and the pseudo-second-order model were shown in the Supporting Information. The results show that the adsorption of QDs on Goe and Goe-HA/FA was more consistent with the pseudo-second-order kinetics, indicating that the adsorption of QDs was controlled by chemical processes. The equilibrium concentration of CdSe/ZnS QDs was less than that of CdSe QDs, which was consistent with the results of heterogeneous aggregation kinetic, that the Dh of CdSe/ZnS QDs-Goe-HA/FA was smaller than that of CdSe QDs-Goe-HA/FA. Meanwhile, the adsorption of CdSe/ZnS QDs was less than that of CdSe QDs after being normalized by a specific surface area. This was attributed to the smaller size and higher diffusion rate of CdSe QDs, which were easier to carry out during the rapid initial adsorption process [16].

We also investigated the adsorption isotherms of Goe and Goe-HA/FA for QDs. The adsorption isotherms and associated fitted parameters of the Freundlich and Langmuir models were shown in the Supporting Information. Both the Langmuir and Freundlich models fitted well for the adsorption of QDs on Goe and GoeHA/FA, with relatively high R2 values (R2 ≥ 0.932 and R2 ≥ 0.910, respectively), indicating that these two isotherm models could explain well the QDs adsorption behavior. The Langmuir model described a single molecular layer adsorption process, which implied that the adsorption of QDs on the surface of Goe and GoeHA/FA was a similar covering process. According to the calculated results of the Freundlich model, the adsorption affinities (KF) of Goe for CdSe QDs and CdSe/ZnS QDs were 0.232 and 0.155 (mg/m2)/(mg/L)n, respectively, which were higher than those of Goe-HA/FA (0.125/0.154 and 0.125/0.141 (mg/m2)/(mg/L)n). It suggested that Goe was more likely to adsorb QDs, which was supported by the observation that the surface of Goe was positively charged while that of Goe-HA/FA was negatively charged. These results show that electrostatic attraction might be the dominant mechanism controlling the adsorption of QDs on Goe and Goe-HA/FA.

In addition, a comparison with the adsorption of QDs and Cd2+ was performed as well, which exhibited different adsorption characteristics. For QDs, the adsorption capacity was Goe > GoeFA > GoeHA, while it was GoeFA > GoeHA > Goe for Cd2+. The chelation of organic matter with heavy metal ions enhanced their adsorption on minerals [48], and Xiong et al. [47] revealed that the presence of adsorbed HA/FA could promote the binding of Pb2+ to goethite. However, the presence of organic matter inhibited the adsorption of QDs, which was consistent with the results of aggregation kinetics and XDLVO theory calculations. The presence of HA/FA induced steric hindrance and changed the surface charge of Goe to negative, so that repulsive to QDs with the same negative charge. Meanwhile, the adsorption of HA/FA also occupied the surface adsorption sites of Goe, which led to a decrease in the adsorption capacity of QDs.

It is worth noting that IS and pH are the key factors affecting the adsorption of QDs. Theoretically, the increase of IS can decrease the absolute value of the ζ-potential of the mixed system and inhibit the attachment of QDs to Goe and Goe-HA/FA due to the electrical double-layer effect [20]. However, as shown in Fig. S11, the adsorption rate of QDs in the presence of Goe and Goe-HA/FA was enhanced with the increase of IS, especially at pH 7.5, which was also observed in the previous study on the nanoplastics adsorbed by goethite [45]. It could be explained by the DLVO and XDLVO theories (Fig. 3), specifically, the maximum repulsive interaction energy (Φmax) of heteroaggregation decreased with increasing IS, and the attractive secondary minimum energy (Φmin) increased, which increased the degree of heteroaggregation and resulted in easier adsorption of QDs by Goe and Goe-HA/FA at high IS conditions [41]. At pH 5.5, both Goe and Goe-HA/FA could adsorb QDs rapidly due to positively charged on the surfaces; meanwhile, at pH 7.5, negatively charged QDs could still attach to positively charged Goe by strong electrostatic attraction. However, the negatively charged surfaces of Goe-HA/FA and QDs resulted in large repulsive forces and energy barriers, which greatly hindered their adsorption and formed a more stable suspension at pH 7.5. These results suggest a great influence of electrostatic interaction between QDs and Goe(-HA/FA).

The chemical structure and morphological changes of Goe and Goe-HA/FA after adsorption of QDs were investigated by using FTIR, XRD, XPS, micro–XRF, and TEM. Based on the XRD characterization (Fig. S12), it was clear that the characteristic peaks of Goe and Goe-HA/FA were not significantly shifted after the adsorption of QDs, and no obvious characteristic peaks of CdSe QDs were observed after adsorption. The TEM images demonstrated the heterogeneous aggregation between QDs and Goe intuitively. As shown in Fig. 5a-e, the particle sizes of QDs were different after adsorption with Goe, indicating that homogeneous aggregation of QDs occurred. QDs were randomly attached to the surface of Goe, which demonstrated that heterogeneous aggregation occurred between QDs and Goe. Furthermore, the elemental distribution of Cd and Fe on Goe-HA adsorbed CdSe/ZnS QDs (pH 5.5, 10 mM NaNO3) was analyzed using µ-XRF. As shown in Fig. 5f-h, the spatial distribution of Cd was highly correlated with Fe (R2 = 0.89), indicating that QDs were mainly adsorbed on the surface of iron oxides.

The XPS spectra of QDs before and after interaction with Goe and Goe-HA/FA were shown in Fig. 5i and Fig. S13-14, and the elemental composition of Goe, Goe-HA/FA, Goe-QDs, and Goe-HA/FA-QDs was determined in the energy range of 0–1200 eV. The results show that after adsorption of QDs, Cd was detected in all three composites, which indicated that QDs were adsorbed on Goe-(HA/FA). The C1 s peak was divided into three peaks after deconvolution, namely C–C/C–H, C–O, and CO located at approximately 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.4 eV, respectively. After adsorbed QDs, the relative contents of C–C/C–H of three composites decreased from 73.19%, 66.8%, and 56.23% to 72.12%, 63.88%, and 45.43%, indicating that the three composites might bind to QDs through unsubstituted aromatic carbon (C–C/C–H). In addition, the relative content of C–O in Goe-HA decreased from 24.63% to 19.72% and the relative content of CO in Goe-FA decreased from 9.85% to 8.24% after the interaction with QDs, suggesting that Goe-HA might bind to QDs through alcohol carbon (C–OH), while Goe-FA might bind to QDs through carboxyl carbon (O–CO) or carbonyl carbon (C–OH) [21].

The FTIR spectra in Fig. 5j show that compared with Goe- and Goe-HA/FA, the peak intensities of symmetric and anti-symmetric CH2 stretching vibrations around 2850 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 were significantly decreased or even disappeared after the adsorption of QDs, suggesting that the unsubstituted aromatic carbon (C–C/C–H) might play an important role in the adsorption of QDs, which was consistent with the analysis of XPS C1s spectra. Meanwhile, the intensity of absorption peaks near 1625 cm−1 and 1380 cm−1 caused by stretching vibration of –COO increased and underwent different degrees of red-shift after the interaction with QDs, indicating a strong interaction between the –COO group and QDs. Previous studies have also demonstrated the complexation between C–O functional groups of carbon quantum dots and the goethite surfaces [25]. In this study, for the three characteristic peaks of goethite, namely Fe–O stretching vibration at 635 cm−1 and Fe–O–OH bending vibration at 793 cm−1 and 890 cm−1, the intensities were significantly enhanced compared to Goe(-HA/FA) without adsorbed QDs, showing that Fe–O and Fe–O–OH were involved in the adsorption of QDs. It was worth noting that after the adsorption of QDs, new absorption peaks appeared at 1792 cm−1 in three composites, corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO, which indicated the possible chemisorption between Goe-(HA/FA) and QDs. Hence, we suggested that electrostatic attraction or the outer-sphere adsorption of QDs on the Goe-(HA/FA) surface occurred first, followed by chemical bonding or inner-sphere complexation between CO of QDs and –COO, Fe–O and Fe–O–OH of Goe-(HA/FA) on the surface.

Understanding the stability of Cd-QDs in the environment and their interaction with iron oxides is important to predict the fate of Cd-QDs in the environment. Generally, organic matter molecules would be adsorbed on the surface of iron oxides in the real environment, which changes the surface properties of iron oxides and affects the environmental fate of Cd-QDs. In this study, the interaction characteristics of Goe(-HA/FA) and QDs were investigated, and the adsorption mechanism of QDs on Goe(-HA/FA) was probed as well. Under the experimental conditions, the surface of Goe was always positively charged and readily attracted electrostatically to the negatively charged QDs. On the contrary, the adsorption of organic matter by Goe resulted in a decrease in the rate of heteroaggregation as the surface changed from positive to negative charges, and the adsorbed organic matter also increased the interparticle repulsion, which in turn limited the interaction of QDs with Goe. According to the micro-XRF and TEM, QDs could adsorb on the surface of Goe(-HA/FA) and heterogeneous aggregation occurred between the particles. The adsorption kinetics and isotherm model fitting results and FTIR characterization indicated that chemisorption could occur between QDs and Goe(-HA/FA). In addition, the unsubstituted aromatic carbon and -COO groups strongly interact with QDs. These findings might guide an understanding of the organic matter-mediated interactions of QDs particles with mineral components in the environment. However, this study only focused on the interaction of Goe(-HA/FA) with QDs, and organic matter can also combine with iron oxide by co-precipitation in the actual environment. In addition, environmental factors such as light and oxidants may further affect the aging of QDs, and the properties of iron oxides-organic matter compositions may be changed as well, thus affecting the interaction. All these problems need further research and exploration.

Weiyu Liang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wen Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Xuechun Shao: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Kailin Gong: Visualization. Chengpeng Su: Methodology. Wei Zhang: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Cheng Peng: Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.
